inputs.user_text: My apple tree's leaves are developing these dark, roundish, somewhat raised spots. What could be causing this, and what do you recommend I do to treat it?

outputs.reference_answer: The visible olive-green lesions on the apple leaf are consistent with apple scab, a fungal disease. To manage this, collect and burn fallen leaves and twigs, and prune infected young shoots with cracked bark during winter. For chemical control, apply systemic fungicides like triazoles (e.g., Systhane) or contact fungicides like Captan from the silver tip stage through the growing season.
outputs.reference_goal: Perform visual triage of the leaf symptoms in the image, use disease identification tools to confirm apple scab, grounding the identification in visible symptoms, and provide management and treatment guidance using knowledge base retrieval.
outputs.reference_context: Apple scab (Venturia inaequalis) is a major fungal disease. Collect and burn fallen leaves and twigs to prevent ascospore production. Prune infected young shoots with cracked bark during winter. Apply urea (2 kg/acre) at pre-leaf fall and dolomitic lime (2.5 tons/acre) in autumn on fallen leaves to accelerate decomposition. Apply systemic fungicides like triazoles (e.g., Systhane) which are effective as preventive, curative, and eradicant. Captan (contact fungicide) is effective and not prone to resistance development. Apply fungicides from silver tip stage through growing season. The disease causes dark, scabby marks on fruit and leaves with olive-green velvety lesions. Fungus overwinters on plant debris.
